ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general beef steak food product class; no specific sizzling hotplate ribeye steak with fries entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific sizzling hotplate ribeye steak with fries entry. Closest parent category for cooked beef used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 covers beef steak generically; no specific hotplate ribeye with fries concept exists.

allergens: gluten (black pepper sauce may contain soy/wheat)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising a ribeye beef steak of roughly 200–220 g and a portion of French fries of roughly 150–180 g, served on a sizzling cast-iron hotplate.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, ribeye steak, cooked, grilled (USDA SR Legacy 13463) combined with Potatoes, french fried, all types, salt added in processing, frozen, as purchased (USDA SR Legacy 11400) in approximate 55:45 steak-to-fries ratio
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct HPB Singapore Foodo entry exists for sizzling hotplate ribeye beef steak with fries as a composite dish. Values estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for grilled ribeye beef steak (FDC ID 13463, ~55% of plate weight) and frozen/fried French fries (FDC ID 11400, ~40% of plate weight) with a small allowance (~5%) for black pepper sauce and butter. Weighted average per 100 g calculated from this composite. Sodium elevated to reflect sauce and seasoning contribution.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — French fries GI ~54 (Atkinson FS et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2021); beef steak GI effectively 0 (protein/fat food, no glycemic carbohydrate contribution)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Ribeye beef steak has a GI of effectively 0 as it contains negligible carbohydrate. French fries have a published GI of approximately 54 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables). The composite dish GI is dominated by the fries component. A weighted GI of 54 is assigned to the composite, reflecting the fries as the sole glycemic contributor. gi_category set to 'low' as GI 54 is ≤ 55. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 56.8) / 100 ≈ 30.7 g, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 14.2 × 4 = 56.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from seasoning, black pepper sauce, and butter
hypertension.guidance: A single 400 g serving provides approximately 1520 mg sodium, representing over 65% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sauce preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load driven by French fries component
type2_diabetes.guidance: The French fries contribute a glycemic load of approximately 30.7 g per serving, which is significant for blood glucose management; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the fries portion or substituting with a lower-GI side such as steamed vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of ribeye beef (high in saturated fat and cholesterol) with deep-fried fries and butter-based sauce results in a dish high in atherogenic lipids and sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency and opt for leaner cuts or grilled preparations without butter.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sizzling Hotplate Ribeye Beef Steak with Fries is a popular Western-style beef dish served in Singapore's casual dining and zi char restaurant scene. A thick-cut ribeye steak is seasoned, grilled or pan-seared to the desired doneness, then placed on a preheated cast-iron sizzling hotplate that continues to cook and caramelise the meat at the table, creating a dramatic sizzle and aroma. The dish is accompanied by a generous portion of golden deep-fried French fries and typically finished with a black pepper sauce or mushroom sauce, along with butter and sautéed onions or garlic. The ribeye cut is prized for its rich marbling, which delivers a juicy, tender texture and a deep beefy flavour. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 235 kcal per 100 g, with a high protein content (~16.8 g/100 g) from the beef and a significant fat load (~12.5 g/100 g) including saturated fat (~4.8 g/100 g) from both the beef marbling and butter. The French fries contribute the majority of the carbohydrate (~14.2 g/100 g) and drive the glycemic load of the meal. Sodium is elevated (~380 mg/100 g) due to seasoning and sauce. Clinically, this dish is of concern for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or Type 2 Diabetes due to its high sodium, saturated fat, and glycemic load. It is best consumed occasionally and in moderated portions within a balanced dietary pattern.

confidence_reason: No direct HPB Singapore Foodo or equivalent regional database entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for grilled ribeye beef steak and deep-fried French fries in a weighted composite ratio, with an allowance for sauce and butter. GI is estimated from the French fries component using University of Sydney GI Database published values. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 13463 (beef ribeye steak, grilled) and FDC ID 11400 (French fries, frozen, as purchased) in an approximate 55:40:5 ratio (steak:fries:sauce/butter) by weight. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimation. 3. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the same USDA SR Legacy composite and are provided as best-effort values. 4. Sodium is elevated above the raw USDA component values to reflect typical restaurant seasoning, black pepper sauce, and butter additions. 5. GI of 54 is assigned based on the French fries component (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021); beef steak contributes negligible glycemic carbohydrate. gi_category is 'low' as GI 54 ≤ 55. 6. Halal status is marked false as the restaurant (Viet'Spice) is not confirmed halal-certified; beef sourcing and sauce ingredients are unverified. 7. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Snacks' to 'Meat and alternatives' as this is a main-course beef dish. 8. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g represent nutrients not directly reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries for this composite; vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database entries used.

improvement_actions_taken: Removed menu prefix 'BB6.' from food_name to produce canonical English name.
Corrected food_group from 'Snacks' to 'Meat and alternatives'.
Estimated composite nutrient values from USDA SR Legacy grilled ribeye and French fries data.
Assigned GI of 54 based on French fries component from University of Sydney GI Database.
Set gi_category to 'low' per ≤55 threshold rule.
Added all mandatory Singaporean multilingual names: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Populated aliases_by_language and verified consistency with flat aliases list.
Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes, specifically vitamin_d_mcg.
Added three health context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
Wrote LLM natural language description exceeding 200 characters.
Used only approved sources: USDA SR Legacy and University of Sydney GI Database.
Used only valid ontology match_type values: closest_parent.
